Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study, but then I'd just... not. When I did study, it was usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because of anxiety about falling behind. I'd sometimes spend hours going through materials in a kind of unfocused way, but it never felt organized or productive. Being 26 and still struggling with this stuff makes me feel like I should have figured it out by now.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks barely touching anything, then suddenly panic when I realized how much I'd missed. Deadlines were the only thing that sometimes got me moving, but even then I'd often just freeze up instead of actually working. My schedule varied based on whatever else was going on in my life, which honestly was usually just me being too anxious to start anything.
Q3: Usually it was either guilt or panic that made me log in. I'd see an email notification or remember that I hadn't checked in for a while and feel this wave of dread. Sometimes I logged in just to prove to myself I was still enrolled, even if I didn't actually do anything once I was there. The peer support group would sometimes message me too, which prompted a few logins.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but it never stuck. The LMS just felt like this overwhelming reminder of everything I wasn't doing. I'd log in, see how behind I was, and then just close it again. It never became normal for me—it always felt like a chore I was avoiding.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I could just watch them without having to produce anything. I learn better by doing things with my hands, so just reading wasn't working for me. The peer interactions were okay too—talking to other students made me feel less alone in struggling. But honestly, nothing felt that useful because I was too disengaged to really absorb much.
Q6: I ignored most of the discussion prompts and a lot of the readings. They felt like busywork, and I couldn't see how they connected to what I actually needed to know. The assignments piled up because I kept telling myself I'd do them later. I think I was avoiding anything that required me to actually demonstrate what I knew, because I was afraid of failing.
Q7: If something felt immediately practical or if I could see exactly how it would help me pass, I'd consider doing it. But most things felt abstract and disconnected from any real outcome. I needed to feel like my effort would actually matter, and with my motivation so low, almost nothing cleared that bar. The few times peers explained why something was important, that helped a bit.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely pointless to me. If something wasn't required, my brain just filed it away as 'not my problem.' Required activities at least had some external pressure, even if I still avoided most of them. The optional stuff just added to the overwhelming list of things I felt guilty about not doing. I couldn't handle anything extra.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a bunch of materials early on with good intentions. I'd have PDFs on my phone that I'd sometimes scroll through when I couldn't sleep. I also looked up YouTube videos on some topics because they felt less intimidating than the official course stuff. A lot of my 'studying' happened in my head while I was doing other things, just turning concepts over.
Q10: There were times I'd be lying in bed thinking about course concepts, trying to make sense of them without actually being logged in anywhere. I'd reread downloaded notes on my phone during commutes or while waiting around. I also talked through some stuff with a friend who'd taken similar courses. None of that would show up in any system, but it was still me trying to learn.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or readings once and then never go back to the LMS for them. I preferred having things saved locally because opening the LMS itself stressed me out. So my actual engagement with those materials was way higher than any log would show. I'd review the same downloaded PDF multiple times across different days.
Q12: Pretty much the whole semester was hard, but it got worse around midterms when I realized how far behind I was. My anxiety would spike and I'd just shut down completely. There were weeks where I couldn't bring myself to even look at anything course-related. Life stuff—money problems, feeling like a failure at 26—made everything harder to face.
Q13: Absolutely. I was exhausted most of the time, and the cognitive load of 20 courses was insane. I'd log into the LMS feeling confused about what I was even supposed to be doing. When I was stressed about money or other life stuff, the course became the last thing I could deal with. Everything competed for energy I didn't have.
Q14: All the time. I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through readings without any of it sinking in. Sometimes I'd be logged in for an hour and realize I'd just been staring at the screen, too anxious to actually process anything. Being present and being engaged are completely different things.
Q15: Constantly. I knew assignments were important, but the thought of starting them triggered so much anxiety that I'd just avoid them. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, every single day. It's not that I didn't care—I cared so much that the fear of doing it badly paralyzed me. Avoidance became my default coping mechanism.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that sometimes broke through my avoidance, but often I'd still miss them. I'd watch the deadline approach with this sick feeling, unable to make myself act. Sometimes I'd do a frantic last-minute attempt, which is probably why my assignment scores were decent when I actually submitted. But mostly deadlines just added to my anxiety.
Q17: I barely got any feedback because I barely submitted anything. When I did get feedback, it was hard to process because I was already so down on myself. Positive feedback from peers in the support group helped a little—it made me feel less invisible. But official course feedback felt like it was pointing out failures I already knew about.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many courses running at once. I couldn't keep track of what was due when or what I should prioritize. A clearer, simpler structure might have helped me feel less lost. The way things were set up, I always felt like I was drowning and didn't know which direction to swim.
Q19: Having so many different components made engagement harder because I never knew where to focus my limited energy. The peer support aspect was one thing that made it slightly easier—knowing other people were struggling too. But overall, the course design assumed a level of self-direction and motivation that I just didn't have. I needed more scaffolding.
Q20: I think that's exactly what happened to me sometimes. I'd be logged in, clicking around, watching videos—but not actually submitting work because I was too anxious about it being judged. High activity can mean someone is trying but struggling, or just going through the motions without being able to commit to anything. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That makes sense to me because a lot of learning can happen outside the system. Someone might be studying with downloaded materials, or they're just efficient and don't need to spend hours clicking around. For me, I did some learning offline that probably helped me scrape by on exams, even though my LMS activity was minimal.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with peers outside the platform. They also can't capture the emotional barriers that prevent engagement—the anxiety, the avoidance, the paralysis.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just going through motions—it's being mentally present and invested. For me, real engagement would mean not feeling dread every time I think about the course. It would mean wanting to learn, not just trying to survive.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how hard I was fighting just to do the bare minimum. It wouldn't show the panic attacks before logging in, or the hours spent paralyzed by anxiety. It also wouldn't show that I actually did learn some things through unconventional means. The numbers make me look like I don't care, but the truth is I cared too much and it broke me.
